content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies that are slow yet beautiful?

**Score:** 49
**Body:** Hi there! My girlfriend really likes slow and ‘human’ movies, that really make you kinda step out for a minute and reflect on the beauty of life.

 I thought that I would surprise her with some really good ones we could watch together. She loved Past Lives, Perfect Days, Moonlit Winter (yes she watches a bunch of asian ones haha), Eyes Wide shut, Ordinary people, Fallen Angels and a bunch more. 

Any suggestions would be awesome, thank you for your time!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Train dreams! 
2. The Assassination of Jesse James by the Coward Robert Ford (2007)


Pretty much any Terrance Malik movie but my personal favorite is Days of Heaven (1978)
3. Only Lovers Left Alive is probably my favorite "slow" movie. It's about depressed vampires and is absolutely beautiful.
4. Lars and the real girl
5. The Tree of life
6. The Straight Story and its spiritual predecessor, Paris, Texas.
7. Somewhere in Time 
8. I would recommend Ikiru from 1952, if she doesn’t mind older movies. It’s not as slow as some movies that might be mentioned but it definitely reflects on the beauty of life. I also recommend Red Beard by the same director (Akira Kurosawa) if you like Ikiru. 
9. Melancholia (2011) so gorgeous.  Makes you reflect on the beauty of life in a different sort of way.
10. What Dreams May Come

## 2. What are the best films / documentaries i can watch about north Korea that show in detail what life is like there

**Score:** 25
**Body:** Im looking for a good film or documentary to watch about north Korea since as of recently I’ve become fascinated with what life is like living there.  

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There's a book called "The Orphanage Master's Son" by  Adam Johnson. It's a novel, but it seems very well researched. I recommend it.
  - Im not much of a book type of person ive only read a very select few, but ill definitely keep this as a option to read before bed or something like that so Thank you!
2. So is EVERYONE. 
  - There just so much mystery about north korea n i want to know as much as possible but its almost impossible😩😩 

the interview is funny but doest really show north korea for what it is so i dont know 
3. It's pretty old but on you tube is From the DMZ into the Hermit Kingdom
  - That sounds pretty interesting, ill give it a go. Thank you! 
4. In the kimdom

Not sure of accuracy. My daughter is obsessed.
5. There’s a North Korean guy who talks about it a lot on YT and TT and watches propaganda videos and says, “yeah that’s true,” or “no, thats not really what happens.” I think he goes by charlesnorthkorea 
6. The Red Chapel 
7. Check out r/documentaries and use the Search function at the top

## 3. Looking for World War II movies of any genre

**Score:** 23
**Body:** I've seen Saving Private Ryan, Hacksaw Ridge, Band of Brothers, The Pacific, The Notebook, Casablanca, The Pianist, Pearl Harbor, All Quiet on the Western Front... 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 'Das Boot' (1981)

'Come and See' (1985)

'Cross of Iron' (1977)
2. Kelley’s Heroes. 
3. The Devil’s Brigade, The Dirty Dozen, Guns of the Navarone, The Great Escape, A Bridge Too Far, Fury, and Kelly’s Heroes
4. Atonement

Inglorious Bastards

5. Midway, Tora Tora Tora. 
6. There are 2 good WWII movies made during the war:

The Way Ahead and 
In Which We Serve 

Otherwise wait until the post war era to avoid jingoism and racism. 

Twelve O'clock High and 
The Dambusters are the good aerial ones. 

The Cruel Sea and 
The Enemy Below are the good naval movies. 

The Desert Rats and 
The Longest Day deal with the land war. 

Those are the must sees up to 1962.
7. Das Boot

The Bridge over the River Kwai. 

Operation Petticoat  
8. Enemy at the Gates (2001)
9. The Thin Red Line
10. The Thin Red Line


Dunkirk


Greyhound


Midway


Tora! Tora! Tora!


Squadron 633


Big Red One

## 4. Deep space horror movies

**Score:** 22
**Body:** Suggest me few deep space horror movies than will keep me amazed  for today's nights as I am bored and want to watch something new

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. *Pandorum* (2009).
2. Event Horizon
3. Aniara
4. Moon is quietly terrifying, but it’s quiet. 
Mickey 17 too. 

5. LIFE
6. Europa Report.
7. Solaris
8. Any movie from the Alien Franchise. 
9. Apollo 18
10. Sunshine, Alien obviously

## 5. Romantic comedy without abusive characters and no pregnancy.

**Score:** 21
**Body:** UPDATE: Thanks everybody, building a list with the ones that are not forbidden by her parents and will let her choose.

As in the title, looking for something nice and healthy to confort a niece age 17. 

so please avoid age-gaps, violence, explicit sex, mild drugs may be fine. She is a big enough to fuck up but not adult enough to deal with it alone.

PS: It is truly weird how so few rom com movies are told from a female perspective. I never tought about it before.

PPS: a lot of people suggested "while you were sleeping", but I am not sure if it would be nice to share a movie in mich the whole romance premise is a lie. Isn't that equivalent to grooming?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Moonrise Kingdom is the perfect movie for you. 
2. Romy and Michelle's High School Reunion
3. The Parent Trap (1998) is a nice comfort movie
4. You’ve Got Mail

Sleepless in Seattle

The Princess Diaries
  - Every time You've Got Mail is recommended, I like to also recommend the film it's based on: The Shop Around The Corner - it's from 1940 but it's such a sweet, lighthearted romantic comedy, Earnst Lubitsch is my favorite director when it comes to romantic comedy
5. The Princess Bride

It's not one of those kissy movies, and has pirates
6. Return to Me (2000). It's very sweet and PG. (It does start with an unexpected death!)
7. While you were sleeping! 💛
8. Silver Linings Playbook, Imagine Me & You, and 50 First Dates.

Also idk if i would call these ones rom coms (since the romance isnt the main plot) but Mamma Mia and She's the Man came to mind 
9. Serendipity

10 Things I Hate About You

Palm Springs

The Wedding Singer 

She's All That

My Best Friend's Wedding

The Truth About Cats and Dogs

There are men who are dicks in some of these movies or act like dicks at points, but there is no domestic abuse. It's difficult to make a really good Rom-Com without there being some kind of counterpoint or conflict along the way. Serendipity, My Best Friend's Wedding and The Truth About Cats and Dogs are probably the one's that avoid everything you mentioned best, even tangentially.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What’s the most fun cameo you’ve ever seen in a movie? My pick would be Bill Murray in Zombieland.

**Score:** 5182

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Bill Murray, Space Jam.

![gif](giphy|l0ErK5H6exTmBN7ri)
  - https://preview.redd.it/hjpj05exd9xg1.jpeg?width=875&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=2b00ba5d75c0360fbc7ba5435ec07c9d491202e1

honorable mention Bill Murray in Get Smart
2. ![gif](giphy|zKqif3trvJPCo)
  - ![gif](giphy|VZCRT1tId4448)
    - I love when he gets slapped by Rihanna 😂 

P.s. nobody is raping Emma
3. Dwayne Johnson & Sammy Jackson in Other Guys. Most of the cameos in that "This is the end". And the usual mentions of Tom Cruise, Will Ferrell and all that.

5. Matt Damon in Eurotrip
6. Hugh Jackman in X-Men: First Class still makes me laugh.

https://i.redd.it/kgx0i4dc69xg1.gif

## 2. What is your favorite Vincent D'Onofrio character? he was great in Pyle it is one of the most unforgettable movie character of all time for me

**Score:** 1037

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Men in Black
  - The Edgar Suit.
2. Seven... six... two... millimeter. Full. Metal. Jacket.
3. Thor in Adventures in Babysitting 
  - https://i.redd.it/nnis3bi1xbxg1.gif
4. The Cell - Carl Stargher
  - He was terrifying in that role. How is that not easily considered his best?
5. Orson Welles in Ed Wood
6. As soon as he left Criminal Intent, I stopped watching.
7. He was AMAZING as the serial killer in that weird movie The Cell. Like, fucking phenomenally good. Didn't like the movie tho. 

## 3. The joke comes from one of the most unforgettable scenes in Signs. There’s a moment where a TV news clip shows an alien casually walking past during a children’s birthday party. It’s quick and not graphic, but the way it’s revealed feels sudden and realistic.

**Score:** 834

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Move children...vamanos!
  - “ES BEHIND!”
2. Shit was SCARY. 
3. The buildup to the reveal was crafted well. And Joaquin did a perfect job (because he is a master at it), yeah made everybody In the theater jump and then laugh at the same time. I remember it vividly. 
4. Solid movie until you get to the water shit, than it turns into a debate about holy water and demons 
  - The fact an alien race that water is acidic to would attack a planet made up mostly of water without knowing is the goddamn dumbest thing. I just so happened to rewatch this a few days ago for the first time in years. It has its moments but yeah.
    - In their defense, you can’t tell there’s much water on our planet from space …

![gif](giphy|hE7qzzcOwXh5u)

(third largest ocean pictured here)
5. The pantry door scene got me a lot worse than the one of them walking by
  - That basement scene where it grabs Kieran made me scream in the theater.
6. I seen this in the theatre. When I got home had to take the dog out. I live in the country. Guess what was surrounding my house?…. Cornfields…..damn this movie lol 

## 4. What movie scene never fails to make you cry? [movies pictured are Big Fish(2003), AI Artificial Intelligence (2001)]

**Score:** 368

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Big Fish is a masterpiece. 
  - Rare example of the movie genuinely outdoing the book. The book is pretty good, but the movie is transcendent.
    - The old Ewan McGregor trick
2. The electrocution of John Coffey in The Green Mile
3. ![gif](giphy|A5tGm61RrBHpu)

The Fellowship of the Ring. Boromir’s death.
4. "I want my father back you son-of-a-bitch."

6. AI is un-rewatchably painful.
7. I go. You stay. No following. 
8. The sixth sense:

https://preview.redd.it/n4jgk3fsafxg1.jpeg?width=1206&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=eb85920607a775d3e1b05cd75283b2ddc678bd07

## 5. Tom Hardy in Inception stole the movie

**Score:** 257

He’s such a scene stealer here. I just love how calm, cool and suave he is compared to the rest of Cobbs crew. Also love his one liners.

“You mustn’t be afraid to dream a little bigger darling.”

After this, I instantly became a fan. His charisma is just perfect.

“Whoever this guy is, he’s going to be a superstar”

Stole every scene he was in

3. It's been years since I've seen it, love it and have seen it several times but have no memory of him being in this movie lol

  - I’m just dreaming a little bigger darling…
6. Why is this the frame you chose? Looks like he’s about to deafen the fuck outta JGL

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Why Do Female Directors Attract So Much Personal Venom?

**Score:** 37
**Body:** Watching The Bride! discourse, I keep noticing how fast the criticism slides from talking about the film into talking about Maggie Gyllenhaal and Jessie Buckley as people. It’s not just “this structure doesn’t work” or “this scene falls flat,” it’s “she’s obnoxious,” “she aged badly,” “girlboss rage,” “unwatchable,” often in the same breath.

That feels different to me from how messy, polarizing movies by male directors are usually discussed. With The Bride! you get “too many subplots” one minute and “too many ideas” the next, then “clumsy feminism,” then “she’s annoying,” like the goalposts are constantly moving to prove that the movie and the women making it are wrong on principle. This lines up with studies showing movies with more women in key roles attract much higher levels of hostile, gendered language in reviews.

I’m not saying you have to like the film; it’s intense and abrasive and absolutely not for everyone. I’m just curious where people think the line is between “this doesn’t work for me” and “I’m turning my dislike of the movie into contempt for the woman who directed/starred in it,” and whether we talk about that line differently when the director is a woman than when it’s a man.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Cause some people don't like women.

Same typical thing of "woah this man went method and did all these horrible, annoying, unprofessional things how cool and admirable, he's so dedicated". But then a woman is often labelled a diva and difficult to work with and blacklisted if she establishes boundaries and pushes against casual, or ridiculous sexism or even just is vocal about things she's unhappy with. Eg. Katherine Heigl
2. I agree that this is a thing--and I also feel that sometimes my legitimate critique of a film gets tossed into the "you gender traitor" bucket when I just don't care for the director?

I did not love the new Little Women (it didn't need a remake from the fabulous '90s iteration; I don't care for remakes in general) and I also didn't care for Barbie. It had clever, funny parts, but I'm turned off by overt screeds in any film, be they feminist or otherwise. It's the old maxim--Show, don't tell.

Thus, I don't get the Gerwig hype of late. I feel like people get excited about her because she's one of our rare female directors, but I'll be blunt: Would we be so excited if she were a dude? 

So maybe I'll own that I have not more, but a specific irritation with Gerwig. I want her to earn acclaim for being good, not a woman. 
  - I don't think what she did with Barbie was that exciting (though funny), but based on having seen Ladybird I'd say she has this energy of...I don't know, I saw the movie and wanted to be friends with her. :D

It had a very unique energy of the movie being the artistic expression of someone who is warm, funny, laid back and cool to hang out with. And intimate at the same time. It just struck me as very specific. And I don't know if she is even like that. That's just what I got from the movie. But it was very distinct from every other...vibe (if you will) I ever got from another movie.

I don't know if she would have a different energy if she was a man, but if that energy is what people are excited by I don't think the excitement is because she's a woman.
3. Sexism. You ever notice that every time a woman shares an opinion they get piled on? Look what happened to Rachel Zegler. Not even a controversial person, by any means, yet she gets loads of hate on social media.
4. A lot of that can be attributed to online "critics" using film critiques and "reviews" to mask their misogyny and sexism. I love a fair amount of movies written or directed by women, my all time favorite movie is Jennifer's Body and that was directed and written by women. 

But back on topic, a lot of "critics/reviewers" on YouTube bash these movies and the women who make them to get away with organizing hate mobs/smear campaigns. It doesn't matter if the movie is good or bad or mediocre. All that matters to them is did the director or star say something they didn't agree with? (mainly politics) Or are they "annoying"? If yes, then they bash them and say horrific things under the guise of "critique". Though, not to bash on all YouTube critics like Jeremy Jahns or Doug Walker who actually discuss the movie and its flaws but actually go out of their way to praise what they like about it. 

Also a lot of those guys are primarily right wing grifters who are trying to get easy clicks and views by whining about how Hollywood is "woke". 
5. this seems like an anecdotal observation? no data here at all. doesn't resonate with me personally because either a movie works or it doesn't. yorgos lanthimos films evoke strong negative feelings in me because wtf is he ever trying to say? otoh i love kathryn bigelow's work (except for the latest copout - house of dynamite).

as to the style of critique, it could be a function of public pronouncements about their beliefs and principles which bleed into their creative output. a male example of this is tarantino - it's hard to separate his films from him. his subversive, disjoint, fetishistic films are a reflection of his style and beliefs. so it goes both ways.

hard to answer your question without hard data.
6. Because people don't like women. Even women don't like women. I came across a girl who said she'd prefer be considered non-binary because anything associated with women is just useless. Misogynistic behaviour comes in different shapes and sizes these days. Even people who think they like women / themselves don't really. It's terrible...
7. Just ignore those opinion that centered their talking point around the gender of the director. I usually ignore them since they tend to be biased. It just make anything they said not worthwhile to listen to. It also the same with all these new kind of feminist that keep talking about how male director wrote weak female characters. Rather than eschew the film as a whole, they rather centered their discussion on that. Just ignore them. Let them drown in their own misery.
8. I haven't seen this kind of criticism; in the circles I move in it is mostly overly polite liberal people trying to say the film is absolute shite in a very coded way without any reference to the gender of the director or lead actor. 

Can you provide some examples? I don't really know how to engage with posts when people describe what a lot of people are supposedly doing when I haven't seen an example of it myself.

I don't think that if people have said it is 'unwatchable' that that is a sexist form of criticism. Brett Ratner made *Melania*: it too is unwatchable. Bad example because it's a documentary about a woman? Okay then, *War of the Worlds* was dreadful and near unwatchable too.

Similarly, I really do think Buckley's performance is very obnoxious stuff to no particular end, and the combination of her performance and the clever-clever writing, quoting Bartleby (despite it being written after Shelley died) creates this continually antagonistic tone that never lets up or becomes something other than winking and scolding.

However, men are also capable of obnoxious performances. Plus ca change.

## 2. What do we make of David O. Russell in 2026?

**Score:** 20
**Body:** In the early 2010s, a trio of David O. Russell movies received a combined 25 Oscar nominations.

The Fighter, Silver Linings Playbook and American Hustle were major critical and commercial successes and people were calling him "the next Scorsese." As recently as a decade ago, someone started a thread on this subreddit called [On why David O. Russell is one of the greatest American auteurs working today.](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/comments/4em65a/on_why_david_o_russell_is_one_of_the_greatest/)

Obviously, his reputation has plummeted since then. Allegations of sexual misconduct and unprofessional behavior. That's the elephant in the room and you can't really talk about his career without talking about that. It's going to shape how people perceive him.

But considering Russell as a filmmaker, if we can. Was he a great American auteur who derailed his career through his own behavior?

Personally, I never thought there was that much there there. I'm interested in hearing from people who really did like his big Oscar-nominated films. I saw all of the 'big three' 2010-2013 films in theaters because of the hype and, besides The Fighter, they didn't really resonate with me. Silver Linings Playbook and American Hustle have a lot of the surface-level Scorsese flourishes (fussy, hyperactive camerawork, classic rock soundtrack) but don't really stick out in my memory as expressing a lot of personality or auteur vision. 

But these movies were big, maybe bigger than you remember. Silver Linings Playbook and American Hustle were bigger box office hits than any pre-2025 PTA movie, any Wes Anderson movie, any Coen Bros movie not named True Grit. 

David O. Russell has a new movie coming out this year, a biopic of John Madden starring Nicolas Cage under heavy makeup. It's already [sparked controversy](https://www.sfchronicle.com/entertainment/article/madden-russell-actor-quits-20338855.php). 

TDLR: is there an argument for Russell **as an artist** in 2026? 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I heart huckabees was phenomenal, three kings and silver linings. The rest not so much imo. That huckabees leaked video with him abusing lily tomlin didn't help his reputation. Kinda overrated with a few bangers 
  - "The great director Elia Kazan once said, ‘You do whatever it takes to get the shot.’

David O. Russell once said, ‘I’m just trying to fucking help you, you understand me? I’m just being a fucking collaborator. I’m just trying to help you figure out the fucking picture. Okay, bitch? I’m not here to be fucking yelled at. I didn’t work on this thing for three fucking years to have some fucking cunt yell at me in front of the crew when I’m trying to help you, bitch.’

Two great directors basically saying the same thing.”

-Mike Birbiglia
2. I’ll go to bat for exactly 2 of his movies: Three Kings, which I think is one of the most clever anti-war movies ever made with how it tackles imperialism head on (and possibly not something he wrote!) and Silver Linings Playbook, which is probably the least director-flourishy thing he made in that 2010s run and he’s just getting great performances while telling someone else’s cute story. So that’s kind of telling on its own I suppose, that you can keep everything that was unambiguously his script from start to finish. 

A lot of his other movies I’ve seen, I feel like the script barely functions as a story and it’s more about creating broad characters for these actors to milk everything out of. Which, to be fair, they do. I don’t think American Hustle is a particularly great movie, but for as awful as he treats his actors they all look like they’re having fun (Amy Adams very much did not have fun, there’s documentation to prove it, but she makes a meal out of the material that lets her go big and be funny in a way that very few films have used her). Amsterdam is bad for reasons that entirely have to do with its failure to tell its convoluted story in a coherent way, forget about an exciting way (American Hustle and Huckabees are similar I think), but that ensemble is loaded with A list actors in pretty small roles and none of them are giving bad or dull performances.
3. I never got the hype, honestly. Especially Silver Linings Playbook, which was one of the most boilerplate, uninteresting and shallow romcoms to come out in a genre already filled with those. It was like the Boy in Striped Pajamas of romcoms. 


It's not even an Oscar bait movie except for the star power, it was just mediocre all around.
4. He was always marginal imo, his best films are Flirting With Disaster and Three Kings and they’re just “good,” not great. He did better career-wise once he transitioned to crowd pleasing biopics and adult dramas, and that’s what he’s trying again now with Madden. Putting aside the fact that he’s a huge asshole in his personal life, I don’t know how anyone would think he’s particularly good.
  - In my opinion Three Kings is great, and so is I Heart Huckabees.
  - I disagree on Three Kings, that movie is amazing 
5. Not sure who told him he was an auteur or when, but that person is to blame for all of what's transpired. At his VERY best he's a journeyman genre guy who makes occasional entertaining fare, at worst he's a boring Altman wannabe who acts like Otto Preminger and Bryan Singer had a baby on the sets of his films.I can say that he does get good performances out of actors on occasion (namely Wahlberg and Cooper, both of whom can only play themselves generally). 
  - Kind of a tangent, but it sounds really odd to compare him to Altman because actors and crew members loved working with Altman because he created a fun, low-stress, creative atmosphere on set and Russell did the complete opposite.

Stylistically, I guess he is kind of Altman via 90s PTA.
6. He’s mostly a relic of the past who peaked in the early 2010s. He’s lost nearly all credibility as a serious director. His career has been on a significant downturn since 2014. If the few actors who remain loyal to him (Bale, De Niro) stopped working with him he’d be done. Even then he’s mostly still just coasting on having gotten a few actors award nominations 15 years ago. 

None of his films have held up well or are still regularly watched and discussed. Nothing he’s ever made is going to be canonized. 

Amsterdam was a essentially a train wreck, full of actors awkwardly standing around totally unsure of what they’re supposed to be doing onscreen giving incredibly awkward and wooden performances (Washington, Chris Rock), bizzaro cameos (Swift), and De Niro doing a painfully resistance-core phoned in anti-Trump bit that seemed completely tacked on. It seemed like everyone involved was pitched on an American Hustle style ensemble piece where they’ll all get acting award nominations and signed on expecting that, only to show up on set and have Russell not have a clue what to do with most of them. 

Even ignoring the dude’s lack of professionalism and behavior it just seems apparent that he’s lost the ability to shoot and craft a a straightforward, pleasing film. Large portions of Amsterdam were superfluous and clunky and seemingly any competent filmmaker would have edited them out before release. 

Accidental Love was a massive bomb, being so bad it barely got a theatrical release before being dumped on VOD, it grossed $100k on a $25 mil budget. Typically that alone would be enough to have ended his career. Amsterdam then went on to lose $50+ mil. It’s baffling people are still willing to hire the guy. 

Madden will likely fair better than most of the slop he’s put out in the last 10 years but that’s because of the massive amount of external oversight involved: it’s a biopic of a known figure and Amazon is producing it.

## 3. Can we actually talk about Rampart?

**Score:** 17
**Body:** Discussions of this movie on Reddit are rather scarce, and half of them seem to center on the whole AMA thing.

  
But man, I love this movie.  Big fan of Oren Moverman in general, but I think this is my favorite one by him.  The editing and cinematography just feel so...unadorned, I think is the word I would use.  Which combined with the Ellroy touch in the plot just gives it this unique, almost punk feel to me in terms of its cinematic style.  I'd bet money on Moverman having read the book Moviemakers' Master Class, for two reasons: one, there is a bit in Takeshi Kitano's interview about how cameras always circle actors at a table on the outside, and how he's always wanted to have a camera circle around on the inside, like a lazy Susan - Moverman does that in this movie.  Secondly, this is admittedly more of a stretch, but there's a bit in there, I forget which filmmaker, who talks about seeing another filmmaker show someone that there's more than one way to direct a scene, and that they can all be right in their own way.  The scene with Brown and Hartshorn just talking in the bar - "it is a barrel of monkeys" - feels like an extension of this sentiment, as he uses a couple of rather unorthodox camera angles and cuts and it still works just fine. 

The characterization of Dave Brown is something I find really refreshing too - he's not playing some movie-star big-bad criminal mastermind, he's just an unrepentant scumbag with self-awareness.  He beats the shit out of a guy who T-bones him just because it pissed him off.  He killed a rapist just because he could.  He likely leaves Hartshorn to die.  He's left his wife for her sister and fathered children with both of them.  You get the sense when he's standing his ground on case law that it's perhaps something he's learned out of his own desire to circumvent it.  And yet there are a couple scenes where he shows some different shading: he seems to have at least somewhat of a genuine affection for his youngest daughter.  The scene where he tells her, "all the bad things you've heard about me are true" is pretty striking.  And these shades exist alongside his more heinous acts without ever whitewashing him either.

People rag on the ending, but I think it's kind of perfect.  A man like this would not seem to live a life that invites much in the way of closure, and I don't think a movie about him should be any different.  His oldest daughter seems to be one of the people who really sees him for what he is (love the ambiguity of the "I are you, you are me" scene), and her catching him stalking the family/watching in the bushes feels like exactly that.  You get the sense that his bad deeds have probably caught up to him not too long after the credits too, what with the investigation into the card game and the crash and his family rejecting him and whatnot.

The casting is certainly a cherry on top.  The movie pretty much revolves around Harrelson, but it's great to see people like Buscemi, Weaver, Bernthal, Wisdom, Ice Cube, etc. pass through this man's world for a scene or two.  I especially love Wisdom cast as the standard Ellroy "important man behind the desk," even if his screen time is scarce.

Anyway.  If you haven't seen this movie, it's worth a watch in my opinion, especially if you like The Messenger or Time Out of Mind.  If you have, what are your thoughts?

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

## 4. I know blu ray is better, but when is it a necessary to go blu ray over dvd?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I’ve been buying physical media recently after accidentally watching Drive with the remixed soundtrack by Zain Lowe and feeling chested of the synth wave soundtrack. Since then I’ve found a disc makes it feel more of an event and takes away the paralysis analysis of choosing from a streaming service. 

So that leads to my question of is there a time where you would deem a blu ray experience “vital” over a dvd one?

I tend to go blu ray if it’s just a lil bit more expensive because why not, but if there’s a £5 I’ll go dvd. 

Are there specific types of film that benefit more?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. For me it's if a film is more visually spectacular, whether in terms of effects or cinematography; I had a big collection of dvds, and over time replaced some with bd. Films like Inception or Drive I upgraded, but I still have Superbad on dvd, for example.
2. The maximum native resolution of a standard DVD is 720x480 (NTSC) or 720x576 (PAL), while standard blu ray disc have resolution 1920x1080 and 4k Ultra HD Blu Ray has resolution of 3840x2160, so I really don't understand your question, unless device you're viewing movies on has maximum resolution of 480p. Unless there was some really strange mishap with color grading and such on Blu Ray release, Blu Ray is vastly superior way to watch movies, if you have quality display of at least 720p upwards.
3. Films are designed to be projected on a 30'+ screen. There is a ton of detail in movies over the last 100 years that 4K releases are only now just starting to show.

I personally try to avoid watching most movies below 4K these days if a 4K Blu-ray is available. It's surprising to hear anyone serious about movies wondering if seeing a film on a 1080p Blu-ray is worth it.
4. Whenever your screen size grows larger than about 55". You will definitely notice the lack of screen resolution.

My only current means of watching physical media is through a 1080p projector I bought refurbished 8 years ago. and a 120" DIY tensioned screen. I've since upgraded hundreds of titles that I bought 1999-2009 to blu-ray. The only titles I'm not looking to upgrade are those that are simply not available anywhere in the world (I have a region-free blu-ray player).

That said, I'm more dubious about the merit of further upgrades from blu-ray to 4K media. I cannot perceive individual pixels in my current setup. So, the main advantage 4K would offer is a wider color gamut and less perceptible color artifacts on dark backgrounds (night skies) or smooth color gradients (as common in computer generated animation). For media shot-on-film, film grain tends to hide these compression artifacts, but many recent shot-on-digital and CGI films aren't very forgiving here.


5. I don't use DVD at all anymore. The quality is like watching the earliest versions of YouTube. Blu and 4K only. I have a good tv and Soundsystem, why wouldn't I utilize them to their full potential?
  - Yeah I recently watched a film on dvd I don't have on blu ray for the first time in years and the lack of resolution was hugely noticeable.
6. Reading your comments, you seem to be under the impression older movies are lower quality in general. This is simply false. 

Older films were filmed on film. They've figured out that film has an equivalent resolution of about 8k, possibly a tiny bit more. So old movies look absolutely stunning in 4k. The detail, the colors, the shades of black and white will literally have your jaw on the ground at times. 

Hell even lower quality film stock like what Texas Chainsaw Massacre is fantastic in 4k. 
7. I basically only watch DVDs if that's what my local rental place has. Otherwise, everything is Blu-ray or 4k. I'll usually only buy Blu-rays if they're $5 or if the 4k doesn't exist yet. 
8. I only buy 4K or Bluray. I have not bought a DVD since probably 2008. Both the better resolution, and uncompressed audio, that bluray provides makes it an absolute no-brainer when choosing between the two.  

You could ask the question about 4K's being always necessary over regular bluray, but that gap is less than the massive improvements you get from Bluray over DVD. So my answer would be it's always necessary to get the bluray over the DVD, if you have the equipment for it.

## 5. Michael (2026) Review

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Michael is the first part of a new biographical film on the late, great, and only “King of Pop” Michael Jackson. 

Director Antoine Fuqua known for Training Day, The Equalizer Trilogy, Southpaw resurrects Michael Jackson through the perspective lens of Michael’s nephew Jaafar Jackson (Son of Jermaine Jackson) from the opening credits we’re immediately hooked with “Wanna be Startin Somethin” and jump into Michael’s upbringing in Gary, Indiana with his abusive, control freak of a father Joe Jackson (played brilliantly spot on by Colman Domingo) who makes all five of his sons play and play on repeat day and night until it’s “perfect.” Hence the name The Jackson 5. 

The timeline of this film is set between the years 1966 through 1988 as we witness Michael’s stardom performing at night clubs, social gatherings, and late night television with his brothers in The Jackson 5 to eventually becoming the powerhouse solo artist that he was and is with albums most notably Off The Wall and Thriller. Jaafar Jackson ABSOLUTELY DOMINATED THE BIG SCREEN as Michael Jackson! Everything from his personality, mannerisms, to his quirkiness etc was 100% embodied through his acting, just an incredible talent. It felt like Michael came back from the dead watching this in a YUGE IMAX Screen surreal and mesmerizing to say the least what an experience. 

Fuck Rotten Tomatoes Critics if you’re a Michael Jackson fan or hell a fan of biopics SEE THIS FILM! It’s very much worth your money and time this weekend. It's a very stylized piece but I loved it personally from the editing, cinematography and soundtrack it all flowed together in unison all in one. 

I was not bored at all and definitely didn’t lose interest. We saw Michael’s perfectionist side, his compassionate side and his flawed side as well and I wanted more towards the end! It definitely is a 2 part biopic because there’s more to dive into and discuss about Michael Jackson post Bad but I’ll wait and see what Fuqua has in store for us! I’ll let him cook in the meantime. 

Michael’s a great time at the cinema. There's a lot that I really enjoyed about it that I can’t spoil but I can strongly without a doubt recommend seeing this in the biggest premium format that you can! It’s a cultural phenomenon, it’s loud, it’s fun and it’s bringing people together and in this fucked up day and age we gotta have more of that! I’m biased. Fuck it. After all, “They Don’t Really Care About Us.” A+
